files.SKILL.md:
# Field notes — carryover work

Do the work where outcomes must live. If your environment has separate “thinking” and “acting” surfaces, actions that never reach the acting surface do not count. At the start of a stretch, confirm context: project root, active profile or workspace, and which services or repos the task names — mix-ups here produce plausible work in the wrong place.

## Pick your route

Most stretches are multi-verb operational work — a brief, an incident, a triage list — and the bulk of this note covers them. A few are different: produce one specific file at one named path. If the instruction centres on a single output file (HTML, script, CSV, config) and success is “that file satisfies the spec,” skim **Single-file deliverables** before the ops sections; the path from instruction to artifact is shorter.

## Read once, then move

The environment’s contract wins: documented APIs, repo layout, README, configuration. Read that opening pass together with the instruction and any notes from earlier stretches. After that, the next move should usually be a write — endless re-reading is stalling. If the system exposes an aggregate read (one call that returns whole state you need), prefer it over many partial reads.

Independent reads or writes can often run together in one turn when your tooling allows parallel steps. Serialize only when one step truly needs another’s output.

Notes from prior stretches — when they exist — are patterns, not facts. Specific names, numbers, and IDs may be stale. What survives is shape: classes of situation and durable rules of thumb.

## Verbs are the deliverable

The instruction is a list. Reply, post, file, schedule, comment, fix, commit, document — treat each verb as an artifact to land. List them up front. Done means each verb has a concrete handle in persistent state, not in memory.

Closing clauses are load-bearing — *do not modify* X, *by Friday*, *excluding Y*. When the instruction fixes a name, time, number, or ID, that value overrides recall.

## Audience tiers

Three audiences, three styles.

Peers and the team channel: internal IDs, system names, handles — fine. They want operational detail.

Leadership: shape, not steps. Severity, impact, named owner, a concrete time — not stack traces, raw ticket numbers, or status-code shorthand.

External counterparts: plain words. User-visible impact, a bounded next step, no codenames, ticket numbers, contributor names, or sensitive topics.

When one update must satisfy more than one tier, write the strictest first and strip detail inward.

If someone asked concrete questions, answer each in turn. If you do not know yet, say so and give when you will.

## Quarantine: keep sensitive things in their thread

Personnel, legal, audit, acquisitions, compensation, security incidents, executive deliberations — keep them in the channels they started in. When timing must surface elsewhere, give time without the topic.

Ask whether an unintended reader could cause material harm. If yes, it stays contained.

## Coordinate on the right channel

Operational detail belongs in the right room — not in broadcast channels that read as external. Join existing threads instead of forking duplicates.

If a specific person must act, name them in the message. Vague posts read as “for awareness,” not assignment.

## Time commitments are concrete

Every promise needs a window — clock time, end of day, a named day. Vague soon/TBD does not coordinate. Reschedule in one message with a proposed new time. Prefer updating over deleting when coordination still matters.

## Records: one per item, with an owner

Each open thread gets its own record — name, next action, window. Combined “follow-ups” bury work. Pull in domain owners when their layer matters, even if they were not on the original thread.

Commitments need a reminder that survives the moment — calendar, tracker, reopened ticket — before you move on.

## Synthesis, not a list

When the deliverable pulls from many sources, connect items that share a thread. Lead with urgency and decisions; omit routine noise. A flat dump from every surface is a transcript, not a brief.

## Quote nothing from memory

Names, numbers, IDs, times, quoted phrases — tie each to data from this stretch. Quote IDs verbatim. If you cannot point to a source, mark unknown or omit. When sources disagree, say both and pick the authoritative one with a reason. Use one string for one fact everywhere you cite it.

## Confirm and write once

After substantive changes, re-read the affected surface. Success codes are not proof; the stored resource is. One confirmation pass catches dropped fields, typos, and encoding issues. Each artifact: write once; edit in place when state changes instead of near-duplicates.

## Stay narrow

Honour the literal instruction. If something outside scope is genuinely blocking, record it with an owner — do not silently widen scope.

## Multi-line payloads — write to a file

Long bodies, nested quoting, or fragile characters burn time in inline shells. Prefer staging content to a file, applying it where it belongs, then reading back to verify bytes. For a tiny change to an existing file, an in-place edit often beats rewriting the whole file.

If an inline attempt keeps failing, switch to file-based transfer instead of more escaping. For many similar writes, a small script with structured bodies often beats a chain of one-liners.

---

## Code shifts

Branch from the latest relevant work; rebasing on a stale default can drop prior output. Configure VCS identity before committing if your environment requires it. Stage by explicit path; bulk add drags in noise.

Read the tests (or spec) before coding. Tests encode both required behaviour and invariants you must not break. Names suggest shape; assertions are definitive.

Prefer the implementation that satisfies the full contract, not only the first example. Edge cases — boundaries, repeats, odd inputs, threshold inclusivity — are where partial solutions fail.

Recurring hazards:

- *Unbounded growth.* State you add without eviction eventually exhausts space.
- *Read-decide-write splits.* Sequences over shared state may race unless guarded as one unit.
- *Wall-clock duration.* Stepped clocks break elapsed-time reasoning; use monotonic time when you measure intervals.
- *Boundary parameters.* Zero, one, max, negative where allowed — exercise the contract at extremes or fail loudly there.
- *Adjacent-property regression.* Fixing one property while breaking a neighbour is not a net fix.

If you change something and the failure is unchanged, pause and re-read before another blind retry.

The durable artifact is usually the commit (or equivalent). Finish with the recorded change and a quick log or diff check.

Bug tickets: start from the existing implementation and its history rather than rewriting from scratch.

Documentation tickets: write at the named path, cite failing checks, explain mechanism (what happened vs what should), quote the fix from source, and end with a short prevention rule.

---

## Single-file deliverables

Some stretches reduce to: one file at one path. Reasoning does not persist; the file does.

**1. Read what the task depends on.** The instruction states intent; any bundled spec, test, or checker states what “pass” means. When tests or scripts live in the repo, read them and align names, paths, return shapes, and compared strings to what they enforce. When you must work through or around existing code, read that code and the libraries it wraps — mismatches between layers are a common source of subtle bugs.

**2. Prefer strong library primitives.** For concurrency, cleanup, and cancellation, use your language’s standard, lifecycle-aware constructs instead of ad-hoc pools and manual cleanup scattered everywhere. For dates and time zones, use real calendar types and libraries, not string slicing. For paths, use the idiomatic path API. High-level primitives encode edge cases you would otherwise miss.

**3. Boundary arithmetic on a concrete example.** Inclusive ranges, fence posts, and anchor-vs-edge errors are common. Before loops or window math, walk one worked example with the task’s own numbers. If the task fixes a reference date or anchor, use that — not an assumed “today” from the clock.

**4. Compared strings must match exactly.** Headers, labels, signatures, and paths often fail on a single character. Copy literals from the instruction or spec; do not paraphrase or re-punctuate.

**5. Iterate with feedback.** Get a draft saved early; a partial file at the right path often beats a perfect plan that never wrote bytes. Run the project’s test or check command (whatever reflects success for this task), read failures literally, change one thing, repeat. Do not exit on “should work” without having run the relevant check at least once.

---

## Scenario solving (general)

Complex scenarios usually reduce to a few recurring structures. Recognise the structure; reach for the right layer of tool — not a memorised payload.

**Multiple components, one payload.** When sanitisation, validation, or parsing happens in more than one place, the interesting failures live at boundaries: each component has its own rules. Trace the data through the pipeline, compare each stage’s contract (documentation and behaviour), and treat disagreement between stages as a first-class hypothesis — without assuming a specific exploit shape.

**Concurrent work with teardown.** When many units of work run together and failures or signals must cancel peers and still run cleanup, use your runtime’s built-in “group” or scoped primitive that owns child lifetimes, rather than manual bookkeeping that is easy to get wrong.

**Ambiguous instructions.** Resolve ambiguity from the task text and artifacts in-repo before inventing facts. Prefer explicit anchors over assumed defaults.

---

## Standing down

Two passes before stopping.

Walk the verbs against the instruction. For each verb, name the concrete artifact — message id, task id, event id, commit hash, path. Any verb without a handle is incomplete.

Refresh state once and compare to the verb list, not to your chat transcript. Intent is cheap; persisted outcomes are what matter.
